title: What the Documentation Does Not Tell You About Treasure Hunt Engine — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The Veltrix configuration decisions around events that most operators get wrong and the structured approach that gets them right.

content.text: Most operators discover the nuances of the Treasure Hunt Engine only after their servers have warmed up, not during the initial rollout. The dynamic nature of the events.yml file means that the default spawn points and time windows are often overridden by complex spawn conditions that the scheduler misses during a test run. A frequent production issue that triggers late‑stage research is the lack of a clear mapping between the spawn coordinates set in events.yml and the actual coordinates recognized by the game world, which results in players being unable to find the treasure or the items disappearing mid‑hunt. Operators notice that the provided example values in events.yml clash with the actually available WorldChunk identifiers, leading to dropped drops and a loss of engagement. Because the problem only surfaces once players start interacting with the map, the documentation is surrounded by ad‑hoc fixes that arrive after, not before, deployment.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

The architecture of a Treasure Hunt event in Veltrix is fundamentally defined in events.yml under the “TreasureHunt” section. Parameters such as `min_players`, `max_players`, `hunt_duration`, and `prize_pool_group` determine whether the event can start and how prizes are allocated in prizes.yml. If `min_players` is set too high, the event will never trigger, leaving players confused whenever the server broadcasts “Treasure Hunt incoming.” Conversely, a low `max_players` forces auto‑queue overflow and forces participants to wait in a virtual queue, reducing the sense of urgency. The `coordinate_hunt` flag toggles whether treasure coordinates are buried in user‑generated landscapes; disabling it means players must manually search, which introduces a higher skill ceiling and deeper engagement. These structural choices shape player behavior: a tightly gated prerequisite produces more hype, while a relaxed gate fosters widespread casual participation, and the prize pool description triggers competition or cooperation among players.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

When designing the rhythm of Treasure Hunt events, the cron‑style `schedule` field in events.yml and the `announce_interval` in announce.yml are the main levers. Setting `schedule` to “0 0 * * *” makes the hunt run nightly at midnight, but operators often mistakenly think this means all players will be notified on arrival. In practice, the `announce_interval` governs how frequently the event queue messages are pumped to chat. If this is left at its default of “120” seconds, players may miss the kick‑off entirely because the announcement lags behind the start time, especially during daylight windows when engagement peaks. Another common misconfiguration is to use the same `event_id` for consecutive hunts without resetting the `reset_after` flag—this leads to prize duplication or duplicate prize claims, frustrating participants and breaking the event loop.
Enforcing a non‑zero `min_players` in events.yml guarantees that a Treasure Hunt cannot silently fail, keeping player expectation in sync with server reality.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Within Veltrix’s events.yml, a Treasure Hunt Engine’s foundation rests on a handful of parameters that steer everything from launch cadence to loot distribution. The top‑level focus begins with event_delay, the interval between each hunt; for a modest 20‑to‑30‑player paradise, a 30‑minute cadence keeps the pack fresh without overwhelming the server, while a bustling 200‑plus arena demands a 20‑minute interval to sustain engagement. Directly tied to delay is event_duration, the length of a single hunt; 15 minutes works well for smaller groups, but larger rosters need 20‑to‑25 minutes to allow more finds before the next round. Reward_pool defines the set of items the bot will spawn; a compact pool of ten or so high‑tier gems is suitable for smaller crowds, whereas an expansive pool of thirty or more keeps larger parties invested. Spawn_points determines how many discreet treasure locations appear; five points serve 20 participants comfortably, but 15–20 points become essential when the player count climbs beyond 100. Finally, max_participants limits how many players can engage simultaneously; setting this to fifty for limited hunts preserves quality of life, while raising it to a hundred or more prevents concurrency bottlenecks in globally popular servers.

When Your Server Starts to Scale

Within Veltrix’s events.yml, the Treasure Hunt Engine’s core hinges on a handful of parameters that dictate cadence, loot, and participation. event_delay controls the pause between hunts; a 30‑minute delay suits 20‑to‑30 players, while 20 minutes keeps a 200‑plus crowd engaged. event_duration sets how long each hunt lasts—15 minutes for smaller groups, 20–25 for larger ones—to give enough finds before the next round. Reward_pool defines the items spawned; ten high‑tier rewards work for small squads, but a pool of thirty or more keeps larger parties invested. spawn_points dictates how many treasure locations appear; five points support 20 players, whereas 15‑20 points become essential above 100 participants. max_participants caps concurrent players; fifty keeps quality of life intact for smaller events, and raising it to a hundred or more prevents bottlenecks on more popular servers.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live
Before launching your Treasure Hunt Engine, confirm that events.yml has the correct start and end times, that prizes.yml includes all rewards, etc.
The start timestamp is set, the events.yml has the event name defined, prizes.yml includes at least one prize, announce.yml has correct broadcast schedule, the bracket in tournament.yml etc.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
